title: Hermes filtered traces dataset card and viewer

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [gasp] Today we are checking the third Phase 0.1 topic: what the Hermes filtered traces dataset actually looks like when you open the Hugging Face dataset card and viewer.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: And the first surprise is scale. This is not a tiny chat dataset. It has 3,679 rows, and the dataset card reports 32.1 messages per conversation on average.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: So a typical item is already a full execution trace, right? Not just one question and one answer, but a whole chain of planning, acting, and reacting.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. A raw scan of train dot jsonl comes out to about 32.09 messages on average, with a median of 31, a minimum of 5, and a maximum of 54. The center of gravity is a roughly 30-message trajectory.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [chuckle] Which means anomaly detection here has to look at a long causal trace, not just grade a final answer in isolation.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Right. And the raw roles in conversations are simple: system, human, g p t, and tool. That is the full agent loop stored directly in the data.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: The tool role is the interesting part, because this filtered corpus is extremely tool-heavy.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Very tool-heavy. The dataset card says 18.5 tool calls per conversation on average. A scan that counts tool-call markup finds about 20.47 tool-call blocks and about 19.47 tool-response blocks per trace.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [gasp] So tool use is basically the dominant structural pattern, not a rare extra feature.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. That is why so many perturbation ideas in this repo target assistant and tool-call pairs. If the corpus were mostly plain chat, those rules would miss the real action.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: Now, what does the conversations field actually look like? Is it native Open A I chat format?

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Not really. It is much closer to Share G P T style storage. Each message is basically a from and value pair. Then the tool protocol is serialized inside the value text with angle-bracket wrappers like tool-call and tool-response.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [sigh] Meaning the structure is there, but it is packed into strings instead of already being separated into neat tool-call objects.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. A native Open A I style record would usually expose role, content, structured tool-calls, and explicit tool-call identifiers. Here, the normalizer has to recover that structure by parsing the embedded markup.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: So the practical lesson for dataset builder is threefold: the traces are long, the role mapping matters, and the parser has to reconstruct tool structure from text before perturbation or validation can work cleanly.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: [chuckle] Exactly. Once you see the dataset this way, normalize trajectory stops looking like boring plumbing. It becomes the step that turns a tool-rich Share G P T style corpus into something the rest of the pipeline can reason about.
